structure.title: 1 Introduction and functional overview
structure.summary: This specification defines the functionality, API, and configuration for the "Raw Data Stream" functional cluster in the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform, enabling processing of raw binary data streams. It addresses two scenarios: (1) continuous byte streams where data is untyped and serialization is unnecessary, and (2) Ethernet datagrams treated as atomic units, requiring typed header information and binary payload handling. The cluster provides an interface to manage these streams, with "Raw Data Stream" explicitly representing binary data sequences. The specification clarifies that data may be processed as either a byte stream or a datagram stream depending on the communication context.

structure.title: A Raw Data Streaming interface is statically defined and dependent on the underlying network protocol. The modeling for the Raw Data Streaming Interface supports the following network protocols:
structure.summary: The Raw Data Streaming Interface is statically defined and protocol-dependent, supporting two network layers: IP-based protocols (network layer) and IEEE1722 (data link layer). IP-based streams use TCP/IP sockets with TCP (reliable) or UDP (unreliable) transport, supporting unicast/multicast, while IEEE1722 streams employ data link sockets with subtypes like AAF, 61883_IIDC, RVF, CRF, TSCF, and NTSCF for transport. Secure communication for IP-based streams uses TLS/IPSec and IAM access control, whereas IEEE1722 streams rely on MACSec and IAM.

structure.title: 2 Acronyms and Abbreviations
structure.summary: The section provides a glossary of acronyms and abbreviations specific to this specification. These terms are only relevant within the context of the document. A general list of acronyms and abbreviations is referenced in [2]. The section does not include terms applicable to external documents or standards. All listed abbreviations are defined for clarity within this technical specification.

nodes.title: 2.1.1 MAC unicast socket connection
nodes.summary: A MAC unicast socket connection is a communication method using a socket bound to the data link layer (ISO OSI Layer 2), employing a media access control (MAC) address. This address uniquely identifies a single device, enabling direct communication with one specific counterparty. The socket operates at the data link layer, ensuring data transmission is confined to the local network segment. It leverages MAC addresses for addressing, which are hardware-assigned identifiers. The unicast nature ensures exclusive communication between the socket and a single destination device.

nodes.title: 2.1.2 MAC multicast socket connection
nodes.summary: A MAC multicast socket connection is a communication method using a socket provided by the operating system, bound to the data link layer (ISO OSI Layer 2). It employs a media access control (MAC) address as the destination identifier, enabling communication with one or more multicast recipients. The connection operates at the data link layer, facilitating direct multicast transmission without higher-layer routing. The MAC address serves as the target for broadcast or group communication within local network segments. This approach leverages Layer 2 addressing to achieve multicast functionality without reliance on IP-level multicast mechanisms.

nodes.title: 2.1.3 AVTPDU-common-header
nodes.summary: The AVTPDU-common-header, defined by [1, IEEE1722], comprises the first 12 bits of an AVTPDU, including an 8-bit subtype field, a 1-bit header-specific flag, and a 3-bit version field. It serves as a foundational header structure shared by all AVTP stream data subtypes, ensuring common metadata for protocol interpretation. The header's fields are critical for identifying data format and version, enabling interoperability across AVTP implementations. The accompanying table defines acronyms like MAC, IP, SOME/IP, and AVTP-specific formats (e.g., AAF, RVF) referenced in the header's context. This header is essential for parsing AVTPDU payloads across diverse transport protocols and data formats

nodes.title: 2.1.4 AVTPDU-common-stream-header
nodes.summary: The AVTPDU-common-stream-header is defined by IEEE 1722 and extends the AVTPDU-common-header, which is utilized by specific AVTP stream data subtypes. It is designed for use with a subset of AVTP stream data subtypes, including 61883_IIDC, AAF, TSCF, and RVF. This header provides additional functionality or structure to support these particular data formats within the AVTP protocol. The definition explicitly references [1, IEEE1722] as the standard source for its specification. The header's role is to expand the capabilities of the common header for specialized streaming applications.

nodes.title: 2.1.5 AVTPDU-common-control-header
nodes.summary: The "AVTPDU-common-stream-header" is defined by [1, IEEE1722] and expands the AVTPDU-common-header. It is used by a subset of AVTP stream data sub-types, such as ADP. The header provides additional control information for these specific sub-types. The definition explicitly references IEEE1722 as the standard for its structure. No other sub

nodes.title: 2.1.6 AVTPDU-alternative-header
nodes.summary: The AVTPDU-alternative-header is defined by [1, IEEE1722] and serves AVTP stream data subtypes that lack the commonalities shared by formats using AVTPDU-common-stream-header or AVTPDU-common-control-headers. It is specifically employed for subtypes like CRF and NTSCF, which do not conform to the standard headers used for other AVTP data formats. This header provides an alternative structure to accommodate unique characteristics of these subtypes. The definition emphasizes its role in handling non-standardized AVTP data formats that require distinct header configurations. No additional technical details beyond the text are included.

nodes.title: 2.1.7 AVTPDU-payload
nodes.summary: The AVTPDU-payload, as defined in [1, IEEE1722], constitutes the second segment of an AVTPDU. It transports data corresponding to the subtype specified in the AVTPDU-header. For instance, an AAF-payload is used to carry audio samples. The payload's content is directly tied to the subtype encoding in the header, ensuring data alignment with the AVTPDU's purpose. This structure enables specialized data transmission within the AVTPDU framework.

nodes.title: 2.1.10 AVTP presentation time
nodes.summary: The AVTP presentation time, defined in IEEE1722, represents the gPTP time when data within an AVTPDU payload is transferred to a time-sensitive application. It is carried in the "avtp_timestamp" field of the AVTPDU-common-stream-header. The presentation time is calculated as TavtpPresentationTime = TcurrentGlobalTime + TmaxTransitTime. It excludes format conversion and processing time of the receiving application, as specified in IEEE1722 figure 6.

nodes.title: 2.1.11 Max transit time
nodes.summary: The "Max transit time" is defined by [1, IEEE1722] and is calculated as the worst-case transit time from a stream data producer (talker) to a stream data consumer (listener). The method requires selecting a max transit time that is greater than or equal to the largest worst-case transit time observed in the system. This ensures the chosen value accommodates the most stringent timing constraints for data transmission. The definition emphasizes the necessity of accounting for the maximum possible delay in the communication path. No additional parameters or exceptions are specified in the text.

nodes.title: 2.1.12 Media clock
nodes.summary: The "Media clock" is defined by [1, IEEE1722] as an entity that generates a rate, such as a precise hardware clock operating at a constant rate (e.g., 48kHz). It serves as a timing reference for media synchronization. The media clock is hosted by the media clock provider, which manages its operation and availability within the system. The definition emphasizes its role in providing consistent timing for media processing. No additional functionality or implementation details are specified in the text.

nodes.title: 2.1.16 ACF-message
nodes.summary: The ACF-message, defined in IEEE1722, encapsulates a bus frame (e.g., CAN) with a corresponding ACF-message type (e.g., ACF_CAN). It comprises an ACF-message-header and an ACF-message-payload. Multiple ACF-messages of different types can aggregate into an ACF-payload, which is transported within the same ACF-stream. The encapsulation ensures compatibility with specific bus types while enabling structured data transmission. The ACF-message type determines the format and handling of the encapsulated bus frame.

nodes.title: 2.1.17 ACF-message-header
nodes.summary: The "ACF-message-header" is the initial segment of an ACF-message, comprising the first 7 bits that define the ACF-message type. These types are specified in [1, IEEE1722], including examples such as ACF_CAN and ACF_LIN. The subsequent 9 bits in the header indicate the length of the following ACF-message-payload. The header structure is critical for parsing the message's content and determining its format. No additional details about message types or payload handling are provided in the text.

nodes.title: 2.1.18 ACF-message-payload
nodes.summary: The "ACF-message-payload" is defined by [1, IEEE1722] as the second component of an ACF-message. It carries data corresponding to the ACF-message type, which is encoded in the ACF-message-header. The payload's content is specific to the message type, such as an ACF_CAN-payload containing a CAN2.0 frame. This structure ensures data is transmitted in conjunction with its encoded type identifier.

4.1 Known limitations
nodes.summary: The current solution lacks support for runtime network topology changes, such as service discovery, requiring RawDataStreams to be statically configured on the same ECU as the application, with dynamic features reserved for future releases. Raw Data Streams do not inherently provide safety protection mechanisms like E2E, necessitating their implementation at the application level. Multicast support for Raw Data Streams via IP protocols

structure.title: A **Raw Data Streams** using IEEE1722 protocol support the following IEEE1722 subtypes (specified by [1, IEEE1722]) as transport protocol: **AAF**, **61883_IIDC**, **RVF**, **CRF**, **TSCF** and NTSCF**. Support of other subtypes specified by [1, IEEE1722] may be added in future.
5 Dependencies to other Functional Clusters
structure.summary: This chapter outlines the dependencies of the functional cluster on other functional clusters, emphasizing that AUTOSAR does not standardize interfaces exclusively used between clusters to enable efficient, OS-dependent implementations. Its purpose is to provide an informative guideline for interactions without specifying syntactical details, ensuring compatibility between documents and supporting parallel development of different clusters. Internal interfaces, parameters, and return values are left to platform providers, with additional details allowed. A comprehensive technical architecture documentation for the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform is referenced in [6].

The section does not specify implementation details but focuses on the scope and intent of the provided interfaces. All technical terms and cluster relationships are preserved as described.

nodes.title: 5.2 Required Interfaces
nodes.summary: Section 5.2 outlines the required interfaces for the Raw Data Stream functional cluster within the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform, specifying dependencies on three external functional clusters. The **Cryptography** cluster provides the **CryptoStack** interface for establishing encrypted connections. The **Log and Trace** cluster supplies the **Logger** interface to enable standardized message logging. The **Time Synchronization** cluster offers the **SynchronizedTimeBaseConsumer** interface to retrieve synchronized global time for IEEE1722-based communication. These interfaces are explicitly listed as necessary for the Raw Data Stream cluster's functionality.

7.1.1 Raw Data Streaming Interface
nodes.summary: The Raw Data Streaming Interface operates synchronously with blocking behavior by default, but timeout handling is required to return errors if operations exceed specified durations, with timeout parameters defined per operation. Configuration involves specifying credentials and socket parameters via RawDataStreamMapping and EthernetRawDataStreamMapping, as detailed in TPS_ManifestSpecification [8]. nodes.title: 7.1.1.1 Use cases
nodes.summary: The RawDataStream interface supports three use cases: (1) a client connecting to a non-AUTOSAR sensor via a socket, (2) a server awaiting a connection from a non-AUTOSAR sensor via a socket, and (3) client/server communication with another AUTOSAR RawDataStream instance. Socket connections can use UDP or TCP protocols with Unicast or Multicast configurations. Figure 7.2 illustrates the currently supported use cases and the corresponding Deployment model artifacts required for configuration. Ethernet socket parameters for these use cases are detailed in chapter 10.

nodes.summary: The **RawDataStreamClient::Connect()** function establishes a unicast socket connection for the RawDataStream instance, connecting to a TCP server, while UDP connections do not require a connection and restrict traffic to the specified address. Socket endpoints and attributes are defined in the manifest via the **InstanceSpecifier** constructor, with TLS/DTLS initialization occurring if configured. The **RawDataStreamServer::WaitForConnection()** function prepares the server to accept incoming connections; for TCP, it waits to accept a client request, whereas UDP returns immediately without establishing a connection. nodes.title: 7.1.4.3 Shutdown of a RawDataStream connection
nodes.summary: The `RawDataStreamClient::ShutDown()` and `RawDataStreamServer::ShutDown()` functions close the socket connection for the respective `RawDataStream` instance. Both functions ensure the receiving and sending parts of the socket are shut down. For TCP, the full-duplex connection is explicitly terminated to disallow further receptions and transmissions before the socket is closed. This process adheres to upstream requirements RS_CM_00410, RS_CM_00411, and RS_CM_00412. The shutdown sequence ensures proper termination of bidirectional communication.
